About This Project

What started in a lab became something I couldn't ignore.

My name is So Eun Kang, and I am a junior at Beachwood High School. When I began researching how fentanyl disrupts immune cell function at Case Western Reserve University, I expected to stay in the lab. The science led me outside.

The more I learned about opioids at the cellular level, the more I needed to understand the crisis at the community level. There was a gap between what exists and what people know. Naloxone, a medication that reverses overdose in minutes, was sitting behind pharmacy counters, tucked inside AED kits, installed in Boston subway stations. Most people had no idea.

So I started mapping it. I visited over 75 pharmacies and public spaces across Cleveland and Boston, wrote about it, taught people how to use it, and brought policy proposals to our school superintendent.

📰 The Beachcomber SAFE Project CADCA CWRU Levine Lab
Naloxone

What you need to know — and why it matters now.

In 2023, over 105,000 Americans died from drug-involved overdoses. Naloxone can reverse an opioid overdose in minutes. It's available without a prescription.

01
What Is Naloxone?

Naloxone (Narcan) reverses an opioid overdose by blocking opioid receptors in the brain and restoring normal breathing within minutes. It has no effect if opioids are not present. Now FDA-approved for over-the-counter sale without a prescription.

02
How to Access Narcan

Pharmacies: CVS and Walgreens carry Narcan OTC. Typical price: $44.99 for 2 doses. No prescription needed.

Free: Ohio residents can request free pouches via OhioRx. Project DAWN distributed 290,000+ free kits in Ohio in 2023.

Public spaces: Check AED kits — many now contain naloxone.

03
How to Use Narcan

Signs of overdose: Unresponsive, slow/stopped breathing, blue lips, pale cold skin.

1. Call 911. 2. Lay person on back, tilt head. 3. Insert nozzle in nostril, press plunger. 4. If no response in 2–3 min, give second dose. 5. Recovery position until help arrives.

Narcan found at a Cleveland-area pharmacy · Summer 2025
Investigating naloxone placement at a Walgreens in Boston · Summer 2025
Naloxone Accessibility Map

Mapping access — block by block.

Since 2025, I personally visited 75+ locations across Cleveland and Boston. This is what the data shows.

62 / 68
CVS locations in Cleveland area had Narcan in stock
$34.99–$130.00
Price range found — free options available through OhioRx
Red = Gap
Red markers show locations without naloxone — gaps that cost lives
Safe Drug Disposal

Unused medications at home are a risk to everyone.

42% of people who misuse prescription pain relievers get them from a friend or family member. Safe disposal starts at home.

How to Use a Deterra Pouch

At-home deactivation pouches let you safely dispose of unused medications any day.

1

Place medications inside

Pills, patches, liquids, creams — including opioids and controlled substances.

2

Add warm water halfway

Activated carbon inside binds to and neutralizes drug compounds.

3

Seal and shake 30 seconds

Carbon neutralizes harmful components, preventing environmental contamination.

4

Dispose in regular trash

Safe for normal garbage. Do not flush — it harms waterways.

!

Not for injectables

EpiPens and sharps require separate disposal programs.

Why This Matters

8.6M
Americans aged 12+ reported misusing prescription opioids in 2023 (CDC)
42%
Of those who misuse pain relievers get them from a friend or relative — often from a home medicine cabinet
19%→92%
Proper disposal rates when patients were given an at-home product and shown how to use it

From our community campaign: 90% had never heard of at-home disposal products. After education, 85% planned to use their pouch immediately. The barrier isn't resistance — it's awareness.

Get a Free Deterra Pouch (Ohio) →
Community drug disposal campaign — 78 participants, 80 Deterra pouches distributed · February 2026
Investigative Journalism

Four articles. One crisis. Reported from the inside.

As News Editor of The Beachcomber, I published a four-part investigative series on the opioid crisis. Two articles received national recognition from Best of SNO.

01
Opioid Crisis
May 23, 2025
Despite Declines, Overdose Deaths Continue at Crisis Levels

Teen substance use trends, the fentanyl crisis, and what local health professionals are seeing on the ground.
02
Naloxone Access
September 25, 2025
Narcan Can Save Lives — But Only If You Know Where to Find It

Visiting 70+ locations from Cleveland to Boston to map how accessible naloxone really is.

⭐ Best of SNO
03
Policy & Change
December 19, 2025
How a BHS Grad Helped Bring Naloxone to Boston Subway Stations

Swathi Srinivasan (BHS '17) secured $95,000 in state funding to install naloxone across the MBTA Red Line.
05
Prevention
March 11, 2026
The Importance of Safe Drug Disposal

An investigation into awareness gaps, stigma, and what it takes to get unused medications out of medicine cabinets.
✍️
Series continues

Follow The Beachcomber for the next investigation.

Get Help

If you or someone you know needs support.

Help is available 24/7. You don't need to hit rock bottom to reach out. The sooner, the better.

SAMHSA National Helpline

Free, confidential, 24/7 treatment referral and information for individuals and families facing substance use disorders.

1-800-662-4357
Federal
SAMHSA
FindTreatment.gov and FindSupport.gov for local resources nationwide.
Cuyahoga County
ADAMHS Board
Local treatment and recovery resources for Cuyahoga County residents.
Pepper Pike, OH
New Directions
Nonjudgmental outpatient and residential treatment, including MAT for opioid addiction.
Ohio
OhioRx — Free Deterra Pouches
Ohio residents can request free at-home drug disposal pouches.
National Nonprofit
SAFE Project
Ending the addiction fatality epidemic through prevention, recovery, and community action.
Crisis Line
988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline
Call or text 988. Free, confidential support 24/7.
